coredata.dc:title: Highly selective fluorometric detection of streptokinase via fibrinolytic release of photoluminescent carbon dots integrated into fibrin clot network
coredata.dc:creator: Alanazi, Ahmed Z.
Alhazzani, Khalid
Mostafa, Aya M.
Barker, James
El-Wekil, Mohamed M.
Ali, Al-Montaser Bellah H.
coredata.dc:description: This work pioneers a selective fluorometric assay for the fibrinolytic agent streptokinase by strategically coupling its thrombolytic action to fluorescence signaling using carbon dots integrated into fibrin clot network. Streptokinase triggers clot lysis and caused release of photoluminescent carbon dots integrated within the fibrin network in a concentration-dependent manner. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) imaging verifies distinct aggregation of the carbon dots upon addition to fibrin. The carbon dots are derived via an eco-friendly hydrothermal approach using cucumber peel as the source of carbon. Structural and optical characterization of the carbon dots were performed using different techniques including X-ray diffraction, TEM, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy, and ultraviolet–visible absorption and fluorescence spectroscopies. All experimental parameters influencing the formation of the fibrin clot and factors governing the release of carbon dots upon the addition of streptokinase have been fully optimized. This fluorescence assay offers high selectivity for streptokinase over potential interferences in addition to excellent sensitivity with a 0.017 μg/mL limit of detection and a wide linear range (0.05–500 μg/mL). Successful application to pharmaceutical formulations is demonstrated with excellent recovery and precision. By ingeniously linking streptokinase activity to carbon dot fluorescence, this novel assay provides a rapid, simple and eco-friendly method for selective streptokinase detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Streptokinase
Fibrin
Hydrothermal
Carbon dots
Photoluminescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Measurement of fluorescence quantum yield of CP@CDs
Analysis of streptokinase in biopharmaceutical formulations
Mechanism of interaction between fibrin and CP@CDs
Sensitivity of CP@CDs probe for streptokinase detection
Determination of streptokinase in pharmaceutical samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to extend their appreciation to the Researchers Supporting Project number (RSPD2023R563) at King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Ultraviolet–visible absorption spectra of the prepared CP@CDs (λex = 350 nm, λem = 435 nm). Inset: CP@CDs visualized under daylight and UV (365 nm) illumination, (B) Fluorescence spectrum of CP@CDs excited across a wavelength range of 300 to 380 nm, with excitation wavelengths measured at 10 nm intervals.
(A) Transmission electron microscopy image, (insets are size distribution histogram), (B) X-ray diffraction analysis, and (C) Fourier-transform infrared spectrum of CP@CDs.
Transmission electron microscopy images of (A) monodispersed CP@CDs, (B) aggregated CP@CDs after addition of fibrin.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of the 0.1 mg/ mL CP@CDs solution after addition of different concentrations of streptokinase (0.05 – 500.0 μg/mL) at λex = 350 nm and λem = 435 nm. (B) linear relationship between fluorescence intensity against the concentrations of streptokinase.
Summary of fluorometric determination steps of streptokinase.
Intra-day and inter-day precision data for determination of streptokinase using the proposed method.
Results of streptokinase detection in pharmaceutical samples (n = 3).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Tabulated values at 95 % confidence limit are t = 2.78, F = 19.00.

head.ce:title: Highly selective fluorometric detection of streptokinase via fibrinolytic release of photoluminescent carbon dots integrated into fibrin clot network

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmacology and Toxicology, College of Pharmacy, King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmacology and Toxicology, College of Pharmacy, King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life Sciences, Pharmacy, and Chemistry, Kingston University, Kingston-upon-Thames, London KT1 2EE, UK

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Life Sciences, Pharmacy, and Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Life Sciences, Pharmacy, and Chemistry, Kingston University, Kingston-upon-Thames, London KT1 2EE, UK

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Assiut University, Assiut, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Assiut University, Assiut, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work pioneers a selective fluorometric assay for the fibrinolytic agent streptokinase by strategically coupling its thrombolytic action to fluorescence signaling using carbon dots integrated into fibrin clot network. Streptokinase triggers clot lysis and caused release of photoluminescent carbon dots integrated within the fibrin network in a concentration-dependent manner. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) imaging verifies distinct aggregation of the carbon dots upon addition to fibrin. The carbon dots are derived via an eco-friendly hydrothermal approach using cucumber peel as the source of carbon. Structural and optical characterization of the carbon dots were performed using different techniques including X-ray diffraction, TEM, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy, and ultraviolet–visible absorption and fluorescence spectroscopies. All experimental parameters influencing the formation of the fibrin clot and factors governing the release of carbon dots upon the addition of streptokinase have been fully optimized. This fluorescence assay offers high selectivity for streptokinase over potential interferences in addition to excellent sensitivity with a 0.017 μg/mL limit of detection and a wide linear range (0.05–500 μg/mL). Successful application to pharmaceutical formulations is demonstrated with excellent recovery and precision. By ingeniously linking streptokinase activity to carbon dot fluorescence, this novel assay provides a rapid, simple and eco-friendly method for selective streptokinase detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Streptokinase is a widely used thrombolytic agent that plays a crucial role in the management of various medical conditions, particularly those involving thrombotic disorders [1,2]. It is a bacterial protein secreted by certain strains of streptococci and exerts its therapeutic effects through a unique mechanism of action [3]. Streptokinase is administered intravenously and acts by converting plasminogen into plasmin, an enzyme responsible for breaking down fibrin clots [4]. This fibrinolytic activity allows streptokinase to effectively dissolve blood clots, making it an indispensable tool in the treatment of acute myocardial infarction, deep vein thrombosis, and pulmonary embolism, among other conditions [5,6]. The dosage of streptokinase varies depending on the specific indication and patient conditions. Typically, it is administered as a weight-based dose, with the recommended range falling between 250,000 to 1,500,000 international units (IU) [5–7]. However, it is crucial to note that streptokinase has a narrow therapeutic window, requiring careful monitoring of its levels [5]. Precise control of the dosage of fibrinolytic agents is crucial. If the dose is too low, it can lead to reduced rates of reperfusion in the artery affected by the infarction. On the other hand, if the dose is too high, it can result in elevated risks of gastrointestinal bleeding, intracranial hemorrhage, or bleeding in other areas of the body [5]. Other adverse effects may include allergic reactions, such as rash, fever, or anaphylaxis, which can occur in a small percentage of patients [8]. Monitoring streptokinase levels allows healthcare providers to promptly detect any signs of toxicity or adverse reactions, facilitating appropriate management strategies to ensure patient safety. A limited number of analytical techniques exist for determining streptokinase, which are activity-based assay [9], electrophoretic method [1], fluorometric [10] and liquid chromatography approach [11].

Carbon dots are nanometer-sized carbon-based nanoparticles with a size typically ranging from a few to several tens of nanometers [12]. These remarkable nanomaterials exhibit a wide range of unique properties that make them highly attractive for various applications. Carbon dots possess excellent optical properties, such as strong fluorescence and photostability, which enable their use as fluorescent probes and sensors in biological and chemical systems [13,14]. Additionally, they have low toxicity and good biocompatibility, making them suitable for the use in biomedical applications [15]. They find applications in fields such as bioimaging, drug delivery, catalysis, and environmental monitoring [16]. For example, in bioimaging, carbon dots can be used for cell labeling and tracking due to their excellent fluorescence properties. In drug delivery, their biocompatibility allows for the targeted delivery of therapeutic agents to specific cells or tissues [17]. Furthermore, in catalysis, carbon dots have shown promise as catalysts for various chemical reactions [17]. Carbon dots can be employed as sensors for detecting ions, such as heavy metals and biomolecules, including glucose and DNA [18]. Their sensitivity and selectivity make them valuable tools in analytical chemistry and environmental monitoring. The common routes for synthesis of carbon dots are laser ablation, electrochemical oxidation, microwave/ultrasonic irradiation, and hydrothermal treatment of carbon sources or molecular precursors [19].

Hydrothermal methods offer several advantages for the preparation of carbon dots. Firstly, hydrothermal reactions can be conducted in a closed system under high temperature and pressure, allowing precise control over the reaction conditions [13,20]. This enables the production of carbon dots with controlled sizes, optical properties, and surface functionalization. Additionally, hydrothermal methods typically have short reaction times, making them efficient for large-scale synthesis [13]. The simplicity and scalability of hydrothermal synthesis make it an attractive choice for the preparation of carbon dots [18]. Fruit and vegetable waste have also been utilized for the carbon dot synthesis [21]. For example, citrus fruits such as lemon and orange peels have been used to prepare carbon dots with interesting optical properties [22]. Banana peels have also been employed as a precursor for carbon dots, offering a sustainable and low-cost alternative [23]. Additionally, apple pomace, grape pomace, and tomato waste have been explored for the synthesis of carbon dots, showcasing the potential of fruit and vegetable waste as renewable sources for carbon dot production [24]. These examples highlight the ongoing efforts to utilize agricultural byproducts and waste materials for the sustainable synthesis of carbon dots [24]. Cucumber peel has been explored as a potential source for preparing carbon dots due to its abundance and the presence of carbon-rich compounds [25]. The utilization of cucumber peel as a precursor for carbon dots offers several advantages. Firstly, it reduces waste by repurposing a byproduct of cucumber consumption. Secondly, cucumber peel contains natural pigments and antioxidants, which can impart unique optical and functional properties to the resulting carbon dots. This provides an eco-friendly and sustainable approach to synthesizing carbon dots from agricultural waste [26,27]. Furthermore, cucumber peel is readily available and cost-effective, making it a viable option for large-scale production of carbon dots.

The developed fluorometric method utilizes the fibrinolytic effect of streptokinase on fibrin clots. To implement this method, carbon dots are introduced into the fibrin clot during its formation in an in vitro setting. These carbon dots undergo self-aggregation within the fibrin network. Next, plasminogen is added to the combination of fibrin and carbon dots. Subsequently, streptokinase is introduced to activate the plasminogen, converting it into plasmin. This enzymatic conversion leads to the lysis of fibrin, causing the release of carbon dots from the fibrin network. To quantify the concentration of streptokinase, the fluorescence of the carbon dots is measured. The fluorescence intensity is directly proportional to the concentration of streptokinase present in the sample. Increasing the amount of streptokinase results in a greater release of carbon dots from the fibrin network, leading to a more increase in the fluorescence intensity.

Streptokinase lyophilized powder (2,000 units/mg solid) from β-hemolytic Streptococcus were supplied by Sigma–Aldrich® (St. Louis, USA). Sedonase® vials manufactured by SEDICO (Cairo, Egypt), containing 1,500,000 IU per vial, were acquired from local drug store. Fibrinogen, plasminogen and thrombin of human plasma were supplied by Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). Sodium phosphate dibasic, sodium phosphate monobasic and sodium chloride were supplied by Sigma–Aldrich® (St. Louis, USA). Cucumbers were purchased from the local supermarket. All chemicals were of analytical grade.

UV–visible absorption spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu UV-1601 spectrophotometer using a 1 cm path length cuvette. Fluorescence measurements were performed on a Shimadzu RF-5301PC spectrofluorometer equipped with a xenon lamp excitation source. The excitation and emission slit widths were both set to 5 nm for all fluorescence acquisitions. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra from 400 to 4000 cm−1 were obtained using a Nicolet 6700 FT-IR spectrophotometer. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were collected on a Philips PW 1700 diffractometer to assess crystallinity. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) imaging was done on a JEM-1400 microscope to determine carbon dot morphology and size distribution. The surface functional groups of CP@CDs were characterized using an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, ESCA Ulvac-PHI 1600, PHI Quantum 2000 XPS system, Physical Electronics, USA).

Cucumbers peel-derived carbon dots (CP@CDs) were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of peel juice. Fresh cucumbers (300 g) were washed, blended, and juiced to extract 100 mL of cucumbers peel juice. This juice was transferred to a 200 mL Teflon-lined autoclave reactor and heated at 180 °C for 8 h under autogenous pressure to carry out hydrothermal carbonization. After cooling the reaction to room temperature, the products were centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 10 min to isolate the CP@CDs in the supernatant. This supernatant was passed through a 0.22 μm syringe filter to remove larger particles, leaving the CP@CDs in the filtrate. The filtered dispersion was lyophilized to obtain around 50 mg of dry CP@CDs powder which was collected for further use. The synthesized CP@CDs were dissolved in water to prepare a concentration of 0.1 mg/mL which is required for subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of fluorescence quantum yield of CP@CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
See electronic
supplementary material
file.

Fibrinogen and thrombin were diluted to final concentrations of 10 mg/mL and 10 NIH unit/mL, respectively, in phosphate buffered saline (PBS, 50 mM phosphate, pH 7.4) to maintain physiological pH. Equal volumes solutions (400 μL) of the two were combined in 1.5 mL microcentrifuge tubes and 0.1 mg/mL of the prepared carbon dots then added and incubated at 37 °C for 30 min to allow complete fibrin clot formation. The clots were then pelleted by centrifugation at 5000 rpm for 5 min to compact the clot material. The supernatant was discarded and clots were washed twice more with PBS. Then, the labeled clots were resuspended in 1 mL PBS by vortex mixing. 100 μL aliquots of the labeled fibrin clot suspension were transferred to new microcentrifuge tubes and then 100 μL of plasminogen (2 mg/mL) is added. Varying concentrations of streptokinase standards ranging from 0.1 to 1000 IU/mL (0.05–500 μg/mL) were prepared in PBS by serial dilutions from a stock streptokinase solution (2,000 units/mg solid). Standards were added to the clot aliquots and incubated for 0.5 h at 37 °C to allow for streptokinase-mediated lysis of the clots. After incubation, tubes were centrifugation at 5000 rpm for 5 min and 200 μL of the resulting solution were transferred to 2 mL volumetric flask. The fluorescence intensity was measured at 350/435 nm excitation/emission wavelengths. The measured fluorescence for each standard was plotted against the streptokinase concentration to generate a calibration curve for quantitative determination of streptokinase activity. Scheme 1 summarizes steps for determination of streptokinase.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of streptokinase in biopharmaceutical formulations
ce:section.ce:para: The purchased lyophilized streptokinase biopharmaceutical formulation was stored at + 2 to + 8 °C until use and only freshly dissolved samples were utilized for determining the activity of streptokinase. The commercial samples, which contained 1,500,000 IU per vial as a powder, were diluted to appropriate concentrations using ultrapure water. Subsequently, the dissolved samples were stored at − 20 °C until further analysis. The percentage recoveries of the purified drug concentrate of Sedonase® vials were calculated relative to the prepared standard.

The UV–visible spectrum shown in Fig. 1
A shows the optical characteristics of CP@CDs. The absorption peak at 350 nm indicates the presence of double bonds with heteroatoms such as C = O, C = N, and N = N, associated with the n → π* transition. Additionally, the absorption peak at 256 nm corresponds to the π → π* transition of C = C. Notably, Fig. 1 A demonstrates that CP@CDs have their excitation at 350 nm and emit light at 435 nm. The inset of Fig. 1 A reveals that the CP@CDs solution appears as a light-yellow color under natural daylight but exhibits blue fluorescence when exposed to 365 nm UV light.

The carbon dot solution displayed excitation-independent photoluminescence, as depicted in Fig. 1 B. Across an excitation range ranging from 300 nm to 380 nm, there was no observable shift in the maximum λem. Notably, the most intense fluorescence was observed at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm, corresponding to an emission peak at 435 nm. This intriguing phenomenon can be attributed to the inherent properties of carbon dots, particularly their uniform nanoscale dimensions and well-defined electronic structure. The energy bandgap of these carbon dots remains relatively constant across this excitation range, thereby keeping the emission wavelength consistent [28]. Additionally, surface states or defect sites on the carbon dots may contribute to stabilizing this emission behavior [29]. The fluorescence quantum yield of CP@CDs was determined to be approximately 53 % using quinine sulfate as a fluorescent standard [30–32].

The morphology and dimensions of CP@CDs were examined using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As depicted in Fig. 2
A, the particles exhibited excellent uniformity and had a spherical shape. The TEM images, as shown in the inset of Fig. 2 A, illustrate a particle size distribution histogram, with an average particle size ranging from 2 to 4 nm. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of CP@CDs shows two broad characteristic peaks at 2θ = 22°, and at about 40° corresponding to the (002) and (101) planes, respectively (Fig. 2 B). The presence of a broad peaks in this pattern can be attributed to the amorphous graphitic structure of CP@CDs [33,34]. To make complete characterization of CP@CDs, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were conducted. The obtained data in Fig. 2 C revealed specific peaks that provide FTIR information. The peak observed at 3429 cm−1 indicates the O-H stretching vibration, while the peak at 2926 cm−1 corresponds to the C-H stretching vibration [35]. Additionally, the peak at 1613 cm−1 is attributed to the C = O bond [36]. Furthermore, two bands at 1075 and 1424 cm−1 are associated with the C-N stretching and the hydroxyl group's –OH bending, respectively. Lastly, the peak at 810 cm−1 is assigned to the C-H out-of-plane bending vibrations [37]. The surface characteristics of CP@CDs were analyzed through X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), as illustrated in Fig. S1 . Fig. S1 A depicts the complete XPS spectrum, highlighting major peaks corresponding to C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s. In Fig. S1B, the high-resolution XPS (HR-XPS) spectrum of C 1 s reveals predominant peaks at 282.8 eV, 283.7 eV, and 284.8 eV, corresponding to C-C/C = C, C-N, and C = O/C = N functionalities, respectively. Fig. S1 C presents the HR-XPS spectrum of N 1s, indicating peaks at 397.2 eV and 398.4 eV, associated with pyridinic nitrogen and amidic/amino nitrogen, respectively. Additionally, Fig. S1 D demonstrates the HR-XPS spectrum of O 1s, showcasing peaks at 528.3 eV and 529.7 eV, attributed to C = O and C-OH/C-O-C functionalities, respectively [38,39].

The optimization of carbon dot preparation involved investigating the effects of temperature and hydrothermal time on the synthesis process. The temperature range studied spanned from 100 to 240 °C, and it was found that the optimum temperature for carbon dot formation was determined to be 180 °C ( Fig. S2 A). Additionally, the hydrothermal time was examined by varying the heating time from 1 to 12 h. Through experimentation, it was determined that the optimum hydrothermal time for carbon dot synthesis was 8 h ( Fig. S2 B).

To assess the optical stability of CP@CDs, we conducted investigations on various factors including pH, ionic strength, storage time, UV irradiation, and temperature. Fig. S3 A illustrates the fluorescence intensity changes of CP@CDs across a pH range of 3.0 to 11.0. Notably, extreme acidic and alkaline conditions lead to significant decreases in fluorescence intensity due to protonation/deprotonation processes. The maximum fluorescence intensity is observed at pH 7.0. Ionic strength was found to be a critical factor affecting the fluorescence emission of CP@CDs, as depicted in Fig. S3 B. Increasing the NaCl concentration from 0.1 to 1 M resulted in stable fluorescence intensity, indicating excellent optical stability of the synthesized CP@CDs. Long-term stability of CP@CDs was examined over a span of 60 days, as shown in Fig. S3 C, revealing negligible changes in fluorescence intensity during this period. This demonstrates the exceptional photo-stability of CP@CDs. Furthermore, the photostability of the synthesized CP@CDs was evaluated by subjecting them to 365 nm UV light irradiation for varying durations (5–500 min), as presented in Fig. S3 D. The results indicated minimal to no change in fluorescence intensity throughout the testing period. Temperature stability was also assessed by measuring the fluorescence intensity of CP@CDs under temperature variations ranging from 20 °C to 80 °C (Fig. S3 E). The fluorescence intensity remained constant, indicating the satisfactory temperature-stability of CP@CDs.

Critical reaction parameters were optimized to achieve efficient fibrin clot formation. Weighing clot pellets provides a quantitative measure of clot formation and stability. Fibrinogen and thrombin concentrations ranging from 1 to 30 mg/mL ( Fig. S4 A) and 1–30 NIH units/mL ( Fig. S4 B) respectively were tested, with 10 mg/mL fibrinogen and 10 NIH unit/mL thrombin found to be optimal. A temperature of 37 °C was chosen to provide ideal physiological conditions for enzymatic crosslinking activity by thrombin. The fibrinogen and thrombin reactant volume ratio were at 1:1 for a total volume of 800 μL. Incubation times from 5 to 60 min were examined ( Fig. S4 C), with 30 min necessary for mature, fully crosslinked clots. Shorter times generated weaker clots while improvements occurred at 30 min. Optimizing centrifugation speed was crucial to obtain intact fibrin clots during washing steps ( Fig. S4 D). Centrifugation speeds were optimized in the range of 2000 rpm to 8000 rpm. The ideal speed of 5000 rpm for 5 min produced tight, intact clots that maintained their structure through wash transfers, effectively removing supernatant. Therefore, 5000 rpm for 5 min proved optimal for pelleting stable, intact fibrin clots in the activity assay.

To enhance the efficiency of streptokinase fibrinolytic activity, various crucial parameters were optimized. The optimization process involved utilizing the fluorescence intensity signal emitted by the liberated carbon dots to determine the optimum plasminogen concentration, carbon dots concentration, and the duration of the lysis incubation time. Plasminogen concentration was optimized within the range of 1–10 mg/mL (
Fig. S5
A), revealing that 2 mg/mL of plasminogen was determined to be the optimal concentration. CP@CDs concentrations ranging from 0.05 to 1.0 mg/mL were tested (
Fig. S5
B), with 0.1 mg/mL found to be optimal for obtaining stable fluorescent labeling with minimal self-quenching which occurs at higher concentration of carbon dots. Incubating streptokinase with fibrin clots for 15 min resulted in partial lysis. However, a 30-minute incubation provided measurable fluorescence increases, indicating significant clot digestion, leading to the determination that 30 min was the optimal incubation time to achieve substantial clot digestion without excessively prolonging the incubation period (
Fig. S5
C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of interaction between fibrin and CP@CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The decrease in the fluorescence observed when carbon dots are mixed with fibrin can be attributed to self-aggregation induced by the rigid fibrin network [40]. The crosslinked structure of fibrin has the potential to concentrate and trap the carbon dots, leading to an increased local concentration. This forced proximity within the fibrin matrix may result in the aggregation of carbon dots, leading to self-quenching and a reduction in fluorescence. The confinement of carbon dots within the fibrin mesh restricts their diffusion and mobility, further enhancing aggregation. Even without specific binding, the crowding effect induced by the fibrin mesh brings carbon dots in close proximity, allowing for collisions and π-stacking interactions between the aromatic structures on adjacent dot surfaces. These non-covalent self-interactions promote aggregation and self-quenching, resulting in decreased fluorescence emission. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) provided further evidence of fibrin-induced carbon dot aggregation. The average diameter of CP@CDs increased markedly after fibrin addition, indicating the association of the dots into aggregates. TEM imaging revealed changes in carbon dot morphology following fibrin addition ( Fig. 3
A and B). While CP@CDs without fibrin were monodispersed with consistent size, the CP@CDs after fibrin addition showed distinct aggregation and changes in particle dimensions.

The action of streptokinase involves the activation of plasminogen, leading to the conversion of plasminogen to plasmin. This process disrupts the fibrin matrix, resulting in the lysis of fibrin clots and the release of CP@CDs. As a result, the aggregation of CP@CDs is reduced, restoring the fluorescence intensity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of CP@CDs probe for streptokinase detection
ce:section.ce:para: Under optimal conditions, the fluorescence intensity of CP@CDs was measured at an emission wavelength of 435 nm in the presence of various concentrations of streptokinase, as shown in Fig. 4 . It was observed that the fluorescence intensity exhibited a linear dependence on the increasing concentrations of streptokinase within the range of 0.1–1000 IU/mL (0.05–500.0 μg/mL). The linear correlation equation relating the concentrations of streptokinase to the fluorescence intensity was determined as F = 11.23x + 0.22 (R2 = 0.9974). The sensitivity of the sensor was evaluated by calculating the limit of detection (LOD) using the formula 3.3 times the standard deviation (SD) divided by the slope of the calibration curve (m), where the standard deviation of the blank sample (SD) and the slope of the calibration curve (m) were considered. The calculated LOD for this developed sensor was determined to be 0.017 μg/mL.

The developed method offers significant advantages over the reported (Reversed phase liquid chromatography) RP-LC and (Size exclusion chromatography) SEC methods in terms of range and limit of detection (LOD) values. While the calibration curves for streptokinase in the RP-LC and SEC methods [11] exhibited linearity over limited concentration ranges of 0.25–250.0 μg/mL and 5.0–80.0 μg/mL, respectively, the proposed method demonstrated a much broader range of 0.05–500.0 μg/mL. In addition, the LOD value achieved by the proposed method was remarkably improved, with a value of 0.017 μg/mL, compared to 0.09 μg/mL for the RP-LC method and 1.72 μg/mL for the SEC method.

Intra-day precision was evaluated by conducting three replicate analyses at three different concentration levels (0.05, 40.0, and 80.0 µg/mL) on the same day. Similarly, inter-day precision was assessed by analyzing the same concentration levels on three consecutive days. The standard deviation (SD) and relative standard deviation (RSD) values obtained from these experiments are presented in Table 1 . RSD values below 2 % indicate excellent precision, demonstrating the reliability and reproducibility of the method (Table 1).

Maintaining this selectivity is essential for the accurate and reliable detection of streptokinase amidst the complex mixture of molecules involved in the fibrinolytic process. The selectivity of the probe refers to its ability to specifically and accurately measure the fluorescence signal of the released CP@CDs without interference from fibrin degradation products. One primary concern is ensuring that the fluorescence emitted by carbon dots, used as the probe, remains unaffected by the presence of products resulting from the lysis of fibrin, including D-dimers, E-fragments, X-fragments, Y-fragments. Those fragments composed from the following amino acids such as alanine, glycine, isoleucine, leucine, proline, valine, phenylalanine and tyrosine. Effect of those amino acids along with the parent compound fibrinogen was evaluated for its effect on fluorescence intensity of CP@CDs. Also, the effect of streptokinase on the fluorescence of carbon dots was evaluated. The results in Fig. S6 show that those interferants even up to concentrations 50-times more than CP@CDs cannot cause significant fluctuations in the fluorescence intensity of CP@CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of streptokinase in pharmaceutical samples
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed method was successfully applied to analyze streptokinase in pharmaceutical dosage forms, specifically Sedonase® vials with a potency of 1,500,000 IU. The assessment of the method's performance revealed satisfactory percentage recovery values (99.53 ± 1.75), indicating accurate and reliable determination of streptokinase content. The high recovery percentage also confirmed the absence of significant interference from other components present in the dosage form. To further evaluate the method, a comparison was made with a previously published HPLC method [11], using the same dosage form. Statistical tests, including the t-test and F-test, were performed to assess any significant differences between the proposed and published techniques. The results indicated that the calculated values of the t-test and F-test, at a 95 % confidence level, were lower than the reference values, implying no significant discrepancies in accuracy and precision between the two methods (Table 2 ). Consequently, the proposed method offers an efficient and reliable approach for the quantification of streptokinase in commercial pharmaceutical dosage forms, serving as a viable alternative to the established HPLC method.

In conclusion, we have successfully developed the first selective fluorometric method for the determination of streptokinase using the fibrinolytic mechanism of streptokinase on fibrin clots, with the incorporation of carbon dots. Our study demonstrated the effectiveness of this method in quantifying streptokinase concentration with high sensitivity and selectivity. TEM images played a crucial role in confirming the aggregation of carbon dots particles upon the addition of fibrin. This observation further supported the interaction between streptokinase, fibrin, and the entrapped carbon dots. The lysis of fibrin clots induced by streptokinase resulted in the release of the entrapped carbon dots in a concentration-dependent manner. The developed method exhibited excellent performance in terms of sensitivity and selectivity, allowing for the precise quantification of streptokinase concentration. Furthermore, we successfully applied the method to various pharmaceutical formulations, demonstrating its practicality and reliability. The high recovery and precision achieved in these applications further validated the effectiveness of our method.

Ahmed Z. Alanazi: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Software, Writing – original draft. Khalid Alhazzani: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Aya M. Mostafa: Conceptualization, Project administration, Resources, Validation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. James Barker: Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Mohamed M. El-Wekil: Conceptualization, Data curation, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Al-Montaser Bellah H. Ali: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Validation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to extend their appreciation to the Researchers Supporting Project number (RSPD2023R563) at King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2023.109800.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
